title: Factfulness
subtitle: Ten Reasons We're Wrong About the World — and Why Things Are Better Than You Think
author: Hans Rosling, Ola Rosling, Anna Rosling Rönnlund
excerpt: A data-driven antidote to the overdramatic worldview, revealing the ten instincts that systematically distort our perception of global progress — and the thinking tools to overcome them.
authorBio: Hans Rosling (1948–2017) was a Swedish physician, professor of international health at Karolinska Institute in Stockholm, and co-founder of the Gapminder Foundation. He discovered and named the paralytic disease konzo during his fieldwork in rural Mozambique in the 1980s. His doctoral thesis on konzo earned him a PhD from Uppsala University in 1986. He served as a health adviser to the WHO, UNICEF, and several aid agencies, and co-founded Médecins Sans Frontières Sweden in 1993. As head of the Division of International Health (IHCAR) at Karolinska from 2001 to 2007, he initiated research collaborations across Asia, Africa, the Middle East, and Latin America. Rosling achieved global fame through his TED talks — notably "The Best Stats You've Ever Seen" (2006) — in which he used Gapminder's Trendalyzer software to animate UN and World Bank data into moving bubble charts that revealed global development trends. He was named one of Time's 100 Most Influential People in 2012. A noted sword swallower, he would end his talks by swallowing a sword to illustrate the power of thinking beyond the obvious. Ola Rosling, his son, built the Trendalyzer software that made the visualizations possible and co-founded Gapminder. Anna Rosling Rönnlund, his daughter-in-law, created Dollar Street, a photographic database of homes worldwide organized by income. Together they completed Factfulness after Hans's death from pancreatic cancer in February 2017.
